# Gke Policy Expert FAQ ## GKE Cluster's KCP Upgrades Requirements **Question:** I was wondering if there are any requirements for customers to perform upgrades/auto-upgrade for KCP/Node upgrades/restarts (i.e. GKE clusters' KCPs are upgraded at least once a year). If so, are there any exceptions that certain customers may have/how do we identify those customers? Is there any enforcement or is it more of a guideline? **Answer:** The documentation suggests that Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) automatically upgrades cluster control planes (KCPs) on a regular basis for security and stability. This process appears to be mandatory and cannot be disabled, as it is part of GKE's shared responsibility model (GKE Auto-Upgrade FAQ, YAQS: No upgrades in GKE). This policy seems to be enforced, as GKE will forcibly upgrade clusters once their version reaches its End of Life (EOL) date (Document minor version end of life exceptions). According to the documentation, each minor version has a supported lifespan of 14 months, after which it is considered EOL and subject to a forced upgrade (GKE Version Policy). There appear to be ways to temporarily postpone upgrades. One method is by configuring maintenance exclusions, which can prevent automatic upgrades for a specific time period (How to prevent Control Plane upgrade?). For more significant exceptions, a process called "Snowflaking" exists to freeze a control plane's version. However, this is described as a last resort for special cases, requires a high bar for approval, and is not a permanent solution (Preventing auto-upgrades (Control Plane Snowflake), YAQS: Is it possible to have an indefinite pause on GKE upgrades for a cluster?). You can identify a snowflaked cluster by looking for a "Snowflake" status banner on its monitoring dashboard (Check if Snowflake is enabled). --- ## GKE Cluster's KCP Upgrades Requirements **Question:** I was wondering if there are any requirements for customers to perform upgrades/auto-upgrade for KCP/Node upgrades/restarts (i.e. GKE clusters' KCPs are upgraded at least once a year). According to the documentation, each minor version has a supported lifespan of 14 months, after which it is considered EOL and subject to a forced upgrade (GKE Version Policy). There appear to be ways to temporarily postpone upgrades. One method is by configuring maintenance exclusions, which can prevent automatic upgrades for a specific time period (How to prevent Control Plane upgrade?). For more significant exceptions, a process called "Snowflaking" exists to freeze a control plane's version. However, this is described as a last resort for special cases, requires a high bar for approval, and is not a permanent solution (Preventing auto-upgrades (Control Plane Snowflake), YAQS: Is it possible to have an indefinite pause on GKE upgrades for a cluster?). You can identify a snowflaked cluster by looking for a "Snowflake" status banner on its monitoring dashboard (Check if Snowflake is enabled). --- ## GKE Cluster's KCP Upgrades Requirements **Question:** I was wondering if there are any requirements for customers to perform upgrades/auto-upgrade for KCP/Node upgrades/restarts (i.e. GKE clusters' KCPs are upgraded at least once a year). If so, are there any exceptions that certain customers may have/how do we identify those customers? Is there any enforcement or is it more of a guideline? **Answer:** The documentation suggests that Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) automatically upgrades cluster control planes (KCPs) on a regular basis for security and stability. This process appears to be mandatory and cannot be disabled, as it is part of GKE's shared responsibility model (GKE Auto-Upgrade FAQ, YAQS: No upgrades in GKE). This policy seems to be enforced, as GKE will forcibly upgrade clusters once their version reaches its End of Life (EOL) date (Document minor version end of life exceptions). According to the documentation, each minor version has a supported lifespan of 14 months, after which it is considered EOL and subject to a forced upgrade (GKE Version Policy). There appear to be ways to temporarily postpone upgrades. One method is by configuring maintenance exclusions, which can prevent automatic upgrades for a specific time period (How to prevent Control Plane upgrade?). For more significant exceptions, a process called "Snowflaking" exists to freeze a control plane's version. However, this is described as a last resort for special cases, requires a high bar for approval, and is not a permanent solution (Preventing auto-upgrades (Control Plane Snowflake), YAQS: Is it possible to have an indefinite pause on GKE upgrades for a cluster?). You can identify a snowflaked cluster by looking for a "Snowflake" status banner on its monitoring dashboard (Check if Snowflake is enabled). --- ## GKE Cluster's KCP Upgrades Requirements **Question:** I was wondering if there are any requirements for customers to perform upgrades/auto-upgrade for KCP/Node upgrades/restarts (i.e. GKE clusters' KCPs are upgraded at least once a year). One method is by configuring maintenance exclusions, which can prevent automatic upgrades for a specific time period (How to prevent Control Plane upgrade?). For more significant exceptions, a process called "Snowflaking" exists to freeze a control plane's version. However, this is described as a last resort for special cases, requires a high bar for approval, and is not a permanent solution (Preventing auto-upgrades (Control Plane Snowflake), YAQS: Is it possible to have an indefinite pause on GKE upgrades for a cluster?). You can identify a snowflaked cluster by looking for a "Snowflake" status banner on its monitoring dashboard (Check if Snowflake is enabled). --- ## GKE Cluster's KCP Upgrades Requirements **Question:** I was wondering if there are any requirements for customers to perform upgrades/auto-upgrade for KCP/Node upgrades/restarts (i.e. GKE clusters' KCPs are upgraded at least once a year). This process appears to be mandatory and cannot be disabled, as it is part of GKE's shared responsibility model (GKE Auto-Upgrade FAQ, YAQS: No upgrades in GKE). This policy seems to be enforced, as GKE will forcibly upgrade clusters once their version reaches its End of Life (EOL) date (Document minor version end of life exceptions). According to the documentation, each minor version has a supported lifespan of 14 months, after which it is considered EOL and subject to a forced upgrade (GKE Version Policy). There appear to be ways to temporarily postpone upgrades. One method is by configuring maintenance exclusions, which can prevent automatic upgrades for a specific time period (How to prevent Control Plane upgrade?). For more significant exceptions, a process called "Snowflaking" exists to freeze a control plane's version. However, this is described as a last resort for special cases, requires a high bar for approval, and is not a permanent solution (Preventing auto-upgrades (Control Plane Snowflake), YAQS: Is it possible to have an indefinite pause on GKE upgrades for a cluster?). You can identify a snowflaked cluster by looking for a "Snowflake" status banner on its monitoring dashboard (Check if Snowflake is enabled). --- ## GKE Cluster's KCP Upgrades Requirements **Question:** I was wondering if there are any requirements for customers to perform upgrades/auto-upgrade for KCP/Node upgrades/restarts (i.e. GKE clusters' KCPs are upgraded at least once a year). You can identify a snowflaked cluster by looking for a "Snowflake" status banner on its monitoring dashboard (Check if Snowflake is enabled). --- ## GKE Cluster's KCP Upgrades Requirements **Question:** I was wondering if there are any requirements for customers to perform upgrades/auto-upgrade for KCP/Node upgrades/restarts (i.e. GKE clusters' KCPs are upgraded at least once a year). If so, are there any exceptions that certain customers may have/how do we identify those customers? Is there any enforcement or is it more of a guideline? **Answer:** The documentation suggests that Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) automatically upgrades cluster control planes (KCPs) on a regular basis for security and stability. This process appears to be mandatory and cannot be disabled, as it is part of GKE's shared responsibility model (GKE Auto-Upgrade FAQ, YAQS: No upgrades in GKE). This policy seems to be enforced, as GKE will forcibly upgrade clusters once their version reaches its End of Life (EOL) date (Document minor version end of life exceptions). According to the documentation, each minor version has a supported lifespan of 14 months, after which it is considered EOL and subject to a forced upgrade (GKE Version Policy). There appear to be ways to temporarily postpone upgrades. One method is by configuring maintenance exclusions, which can prevent automatic upgrades for a specific time period (How to prevent Control Plane upgrade?). For more significant exceptions, a process called "Snowflaking" exists to freeze a control plane's version. However, this is described as a last resort for special cases, requires a high bar for approval, and is not a permanent solution (Preventing auto-upgrades (Control Plane Snowflake), YAQS: Is it possible to have an indefinite pause on GKE upgrades for a cluster?). According to the documentation, each minor version has a supported lifespan of 14 months, after which it is considered EOL and subject to a forced upgrade (GKE Version Policy). There appear to be ways to temporarily postpone upgrades. One method is by configuring maintenance exclusions, which can prevent automatic upgrades for a specific time period (How to prevent Control Plane upgrade?). For more significant exceptions, a process called "Snowflaking" exists to freeze a control plane's version. However, this is described as a last resort for special cases, requires a high bar for approval, and is not a permanent solution (Preventing auto-upgrades (Control Plane Snowflake), YAQS: Is it possible to have an indefinite pause on GKE upgrades for a cluster?). You can identify a snowflaked cluster by looking for a "Snowflake" status banner on its monitoring dashboard (Check if Snowflake is enabled). --- ## GKE Cluster's KCP Upgrades Requirements **Question:** I was wondering if there are any requirements for customers to perform upgrades/auto-upgrade for KCP/Node upgrades/restarts (i.e. GKE clusters' KCPs are upgraded at least once a year). If so, are there any exceptions that certain customers may have/how do we identify those customers? Is there any enforcement or is it more of a guideline? **Answer:** The documentation suggests that Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) automatically upgrades cluster control planes (KCPs) on a regular basis for security and stability. This process appears to be mandatory and cannot be disabled, as it is part of GKE's shared responsibility model (GKE Auto-Upgrade FAQ, YAQS: No upgrades in GKE). This policy seems to be enforced, as GKE will forcibly upgrade clusters once their version reaches its End of Life (EOL) date (Document minor version end of life exceptions). According to the documentation, each minor version has a supported lifespan of 14 months, after which it is considered EOL and subject to a forced upgrade (GKE Version Policy). There appear to be ways to temporarily postpone upgrades. One method is by configuring maintenance exclusions, which can prevent automatic upgrades for a specific time period (How to prevent Control Plane upgrade?). For more significant exceptions, a process called "Snowflaking" exists to freeze a control plane's version. However, this is described as a last resort for special cases, requires a high bar for approval, and is not a permanent solution (Preventing auto-upgrades (Control Plane Snowflake), YAQS: Is it possible to have an indefinite pause on GKE upgrades for a cluster?). You can identify a snowflaked cluster by looking for a "Snowflake" status banner on its monitoring dashboard (Check if Snowflake is enabled). --- ## GKE Cluster's KCP Upgrades Requirements **Question:** I was wondering if there are any requirements for customers to perform upgrades/auto-upgrade for KCP/Node upgrades/restarts (i.e. GKE clusters' KCPs are upgraded at least once a year). If so, are there any exceptions that certain customers may have/how do we identify those customers? Is there any enforcement or is it more of a guideline? **Answer:** The documentation suggests that Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) automatically upgrades cluster control planes (KCPs) on a regular basis for security and stability. This process appears to be mandatory and cannot be disabled, as it is part of GKE's shared responsibility model (GKE Auto-Upgrade FAQ, YAQS: No upgrades in GKE). This policy seems to be enforced, as GKE will forcibly upgrade clusters once their version reaches its End of Life (EOL) date (Document minor version end of life exceptions). According to the documentation, each minor version has a supported lifespan of 14 months, after which it is considered EOL and subject to a forced upgrade (GKE Version Policy). There appear to be ways to temporarily postpone upgrades. One method is by configuring maintenance exclusions, which can prevent automatic upgrades for a specific time period (How to prevent Control Plane upgrade?). For more significant exceptions, a process called "Snowflaking" exists to freeze a control plane's version. However, this is described as a last resort for special cases, requires a high bar for approval, and is not a permanent solution (Preventing auto-upgrades (Control Plane Snowflake), YAQS: Is it possible to have an indefinite pause on GKE upgrades for a cluster?). You can identify a snowflaked cluster by looking for a "Snowflake" status banner on its monitoring dashboard (Check if Snowflake is enabled). --- ## GKE Cluster's KCP Upgrades Requirements **Question:** I was wondering if there are any requirements for customers to perform upgrades/auto-upgrade for KCP/Node upgrades/restarts (i.e. GKE clusters' KCPs are upgraded at least once a year). If so, are there any exceptions that certain customers may have/how do we identify those customers? Is there any enforcement or is it more of a guideline? **Answer:** The documentation suggests that Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) automatically upgrades cluster control planes (KCPs) on a regular basis for security and stability. This process appears to be mandatory and cannot be disabled, as it is part of GKE's shared responsibility model (GKE Auto-Upgrade FAQ, YAQS: No upgrades in GKE). This policy seems to be enforced, as GKE will forcibly upgrade clusters once their version reaches its End of Life (EOL) date (Document minor version end of life exceptions). According to the documentation, each minor version has a supported lifespan of 14 months, after which it is considered EOL and subject to a forced upgrade (GKE Version Policy). There appear to be ways to temporarily postpone upgrades. One method is by configuring maintenance exclusions, which can prevent automatic upgrades for a specific time period (How to prevent Control Plane upgrade?). For more significant exceptions, a process called "Snowflaking" exists to freeze a control plane's version. However, this is described as a last resort for special cases, requires a high bar for approval, and is not a permanent solution (Preventing auto-upgrades (Control Plane Snowflake), YAQS: Is it possible to have an indefinite pause on GKE upgrades for a cluster?). You can identify a snowflaked cluster by looking for a "Snowflake" status banner on its monitoring dashboard (Check if Snowflake is enabled). --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. On 11/1/2023 the customer wants to pause upgrades for a code freeze - they are running Static (no channel). Docs state that control planes will still auto upgrade at EoL is it possible to freeze those upgrades during the time of their code freeze? **Answer:** In case of EOL, the release channel and static are treated the same way. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. On 11/1/2023 the customer wants to pause upgrades for a code freeze - they are running Static (no channel). Docs state that control planes will still auto upgrade at EoL is it possible to freeze those upgrades during the time of their code freeze? **Answer:** In case of EOL, the release channel and static are treated the same way. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. On 11/1/2023 the customer wants to pause upgrades for a code freeze - they are running Static (no channel). Docs state that control planes will still auto upgrade at EoL is it possible to freeze those upgrades during the time of their code freeze? **Answer:** In case of EOL, the release channel and static are treated the same way. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. On 11/1/2023 the customer wants to pause upgrades for a code freeze - they are running Static (no channel). Docs state that control planes will still auto upgrade at EoL is it possible to freeze those upgrades during the time of their code freeze? **Answer:** In case of EOL, the release channel and static are treated the same way. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. On 11/1/2023 the customer wants to pause upgrades for a code freeze - they are running Static (no channel). Docs state that control planes will still auto upgrade at EoL is it possible to freeze those upgrades during the time of their code freeze? **Answer:** In case of EOL, the release channel and static are treated the same way. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. On 11/1/2023 the customer wants to pause upgrades for a code freeze - they are running Static (no channel). Docs state that control planes will still auto upgrade at EoL is it possible to freeze those upgrades during the time of their code freeze? **Answer:** In case of EOL, the release channel and static are treated the same way. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. On 11/1/2023 the customer wants to pause upgrades for a code freeze - they are running Static (no channel). Docs state that control planes will still auto upgrade at EoL is it possible to freeze those upgrades during the time of their code freeze? **Answer:** In case of EOL, the release channel and static are treated the same way. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. On 11/1/2023 the customer wants to pause upgrades for a code freeze - they are running Static (no channel). Docs state that control planes will still auto upgrade at EoL is it possible to freeze those upgrades during the time of their code freeze? **Answer:** In case of EOL, the release channel and static are treated the same way. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. On 11/1/2023 the customer wants to pause upgrades for a code freeze - they are running Static (no channel). Docs state that control planes will still auto upgrade at EoL is it possible to freeze those upgrades during the time of their code freeze? **Answer:** In case of EOL, the release channel and static are treated the same way. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. If a cluster is using [any deprecated API](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/deprecations/apis-1-25); the cluster will NOT be auto-upgraded (or force upgraded) until Jan 8th-2024. If a cluster is NOT using any deprecated API; that cluster (release channel or static) will be auto-upgraded starting Oct 31st.
If the customer wants to prevent this cluster from the auto-upgrade; they can apply Maintenance Exclusion of 'No Upgrades` to defer the upgrades for 30 days. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Can static release GKE upgrades be paused for a customer code freeze? **Question:** I have a customer who has GKE clusters running on a static release that are on v1.24 which is going EOL on 10/31/2023. E.g.; If they apply the maintenance exclusion on Oct 30th; their cluster (control plane + nodepools) will not be upgraded until Nov 30th. --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.

Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.

The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.

Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.

The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community.
