The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.

Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. IHAC looking to set up a maintenance exclusion window for more than 30 days or a long term one (more than 100 days), they tried setting up one but facing this error. Error: googleapi: Error 400: Error validating maintenance policy: maintenance policy would go longer than 32d without 48h maintenance availability of >= 4h contiguous duration (in time range [2022-07-24T00:00:00Z, 2022-08-25T00:00:00Z])., badRequest I see there are multiple limitations [https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#limitations-maint-exclusions] to set the maintenance exclusion but they want to know what alternate options customers can have to ensure no upgrades. how can they setup 180 days exclusion without contradicting this rule You can add a maximum of three maintenance exclusions that exclude all upgrades (that is, a scope of "no upgrades"). These exclusions must be configured to allow for at least 48 hours of maintenance availability in a 32-day rolling window. **Answer:** that 30 days rule only applies to control plane upgrades and not to node upgrades. We will not go more than 30 days without upgrading the control plane to a patch version. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.

The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. Of course with "No Upgrades" we also did not upgrade nodes for 30 days.
Our understanding from most customers is that they were concerned about upgrading nodes (as it can be disruptive to workloads) and concerned with upgrading the control plane to a new Kubernetes minor version as perhaps they may be using deprecated APIs or they are worried about compatibility.
The "No minor or node upgrades" exclusion should meet the needs of our more conservative customers. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## Maintenance exclusions to prevent GKE upgrades **Question:** I would like to share this since I had to explore this with Eng. to find right answers so feel this might be helpful to broader TAM community. The "No Upgrades" exclusion used to be the only exclusion and was limited to 30 days because that as long as we are willing to go in terms of not upgrading the control plane. We don't upgrade nodes at all for 180 days (and then you can actually create another exclusion after that is up that lasts until EOS for the GKE minor version) and we only do patch upgrades on the control plane. and this is the command - https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/maintenance-windows-and-exclusions#existing-maintenance-exclusion For more detailed convo you can refer to this Group post [https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/containers-discuss/c/5Lzo3HbPE9k] --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021. See screenshots below (source). Question is: Where can I find (in code) the CL/change that caused this? Images: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1FJM0Rx2QDRdSiUw6lSf3tq3UwP0swfXf_M-IlaFBK50/edit **Answer:** The file in question is google3/production/borg/cloud-kubernetes/config/rollout_config.textpb. But it's not merely enough to look at when a change is submitted. You also need to look at when it rolls out. These are dynamically pushed but there's an extensive and often slow qualification process (that sometimes adds multiple week delays). http://cl-status/#/summary/Cloud%20Kubernetes%20-%20Rollout%20Server%20Microservice%20-%20Components/<cl-num> is usually the simplest way to verify a CL to that file being actually live. In addition, see #duration for further things that can cause delays even when a CL is live. In this case, kill switches are to blame. We used them to freeze auto-upgrades over the holidays, then even once we removed that kill switch, an unrelated bug had added _new_ kill switches that also had to be removed. That removal is a ~4 day slow rollout. is a sample customer issue about this. --- ## What triggers GKE upgrades? **Question:** There are very clear moments in time in which GKE upgrades clusters in a release channel, for example (for Regular) 1.20.8 clearly was rolled out in July 29 2021 and then 1.20.9 on September 2 2021.
